text: I'd like to thank Sages, John, and Rick for the honor of the podium. I was asked to speak on laparoscopic protocolectomy and IPAA.
As you've seen here, the commonest indication for that is ulcerative colitis, although about ten percent of the time it's also used for FAP.

When we're talking about operating for ulcerative colitis, basically our options are proctocolectomy and a brook ileostomy, or proctocolectomy with some kind of reconstruction done as either one stage, two stage, or in the very sick patients that Connor and John have mentioned as a three stage.

I think as we've developed minimally invasive procedures, laparoscopy, and become more comfortable with the techniques, we've become less and less invasive. So we've gone from laparotomy, to laparoscopic assisted to more completely laparoscopic procedures. And that's what I'd like to talk about today.

So this evolution has been with a very complex operation and multiple incisions with the early attempts at lap pouches, simplified it with fewer incisions. And then when we try to get even more simple in terms of the number of incisions, that makes the procedure a little more complex to perform again.

So the more invasive procedures, obviously open laparotomy. I've also included in this the hybrid laparoscopic procedures where the surgeon will do the total colectomy laparoscopically and then make either a fan and steel or a small lower midline incision to do the rectal dissection.
And some would disagree with me, but I think the hand-assisted procedures fall in this group too because the incision ends up being larger than you would actually use for extraction of the specimen.

So here, just a illustration of laparotomy and the ileostomy. This would be a hybrid or hand assist with the laparoscopic incisions, and then either a fan and steel or a low midline for either the hand assist device or a hybrid procedure.
So if you want to get less invasive than that, how do we progress? Well, the way I looked at it is that when the colon is mobilized in a lateral to medial fashion, it becomes a midline structure.
The surgeon transects the rectum, allowing the entire colon and rectum to be extracted through a small peri-umbilical incision, demonstrating a laparoscopic-assisted technique.
The surgical setup includes a small peri-umbilical incision and two ports, with a third port in the right lower quadrant designated as the stoma site.

Just to compare hand assist and laparoscopic, Peter Marcello was the principal investigator on a randomized trial, which compared forty-seven hand assist and forty-eight laparoscopic and included both sigmoid colectomies and total colectomies. And you'll see in both the sigmoid colectomy and total colectomy, the operation was quicker in the hand assist group.
There's a series of data from Mayo that compared a hundred laparoscopic cases with two hundred open. Within the lap group, seventy-five were lap assisted, twenty-five were hand assisted.

Groups very similar in terms of outcomes except for the operative time which was longer in the laparoscopic group. Their post-op recovery was quicker in the laparoscopic group, length of stay was shorter.

Interestingly, in the laparoscopic group, the laparoscopic assisted cases took almost an hour less than the hand assist cases, which is the converse of Peter Marcello's findings. So how can we get even less invasive than that, than using a peri-umbilical extraction incision? Well, when the colon is fully mobilized and the mesentery is divided, then basically the colon can be exteriorized just about anywhere on the abdominal wall. So then it can be exteriorized through the ileostomy site, and we're not using a separate extraction site. And that's what I would refer to as completely laparoscopic.

Choosing patients for procedures, laparotomy, obviously any patient would be a candidate for that. Laparoscopic assisted would apply to most patients would be candidates for that. The completely laparoscopic and then moving to the single incision cases, typically patients at this point I think need to have a normal BMI.

Extracting a colon in someone who's heavy through the ileostomy site requires the ileostomy site to be larger than it otherwise would be and can set the patient up potentially for problems with peristomal hernias, although that's not very likely as they would probably only have it for a couple of months.

The operating room setup is shown with the patient positioned for a colectomy. The patient's thighs are level with the abdomen to avoid interference with instruments at the flexures. The arms are wrapped and tucked, with hands protected using foam and gel pads behind the elbows. The sacrum is positioned over the table break to facilitate stapling or mucosectomy.
A warm air blanket is placed over the patient's chest, and the chest is taped securely to the operating table. The team performs a tilt test to ensure the patient does not slip before draping.

In the operating room setup, the instrument tray is positioned at the foot of the table, and screens are placed on either side of the patient. This arrangement allows for clear access and movement of the screens between the patient's ankle and shoulder during the procedure around the colon.

Basic steps can be broken up into five easy steps, a four-port technique. Do the left colon and splenic flexure first, then the right side, then dissect the rectum for the third step. The fourth step is exteriorizing and creation of the pouch, and then the last step is creating the anastomosis. Or you can make it difficult like that.

The surgical setup for a completely laparoscopic colectomy is shown, featuring a twelve millimeter port supraumbilical, a twelve millimeter port through the ileostomy site, and two five millimeter ports arranged in a diamond configuration. The procedure begins at the left pelvic brim with a lateral to medial approach.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to dissect along the white line, maintaining a bloodless plane. The left ureter is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon navigates the retroperitoneal plane along the descending colon, with the left peritoneal reflection visible. The white line remains with the patient, guiding the dissection as it progresses cephalad over the kidney towards the spleen, which is visible at the top of the screen.

The surgeon mobilizes the proximal descending colon off the retroperitoneum, moving the plane medially closer to the colon. The distal transverse colon is then mobilized, with the option to enter the lesser sac from below, leaving the omentum with the patient, or from above, taking the omentum with the colon. The splenic flexure is fully mobilized.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to manage the omentum, ensuring the left side is fully mobilized to the midline before proceeding to the right side.

The surgeon scores the peritoneum around the base of the cecum and the terminal ileal mesentery, using a lateral to medial approach to mobilize the ascending colon from the retroperitoneum. The white line remains visible, indicating the correct plane of dissection.
The surgeon uses a lateral to medial approach to preserve the ileocolic pedicle, applying careful traction and counter traction to clearly visualize the dissection plane.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the right colon up to the duodenum, visible at the bottom of the screen. Cold cutting is performed near the duodenum to avoid cautery. The hepatic flexure is then mobilized, with careful identification and protection of the duodenum during dissection, sweeping tissues downward.
The surgeon identifies the retroperitoneal plane around the hepatic flexure, ensuring the colon remains vascularized during the rectal dissection in the pelvis.

The surgeon begins on the left side, entering the presacral space and extending the dissection medially and distally. The procedure then shifts to the right side, where the ureter is identified, and the rectum is elevated. The right parorectal peritoneum is scored, connecting with the previous left-side dissection. The surgeon continues cutting along the marked line, working further down in the pelvis on the right side of the rectum, moving from lateral to anterior and towards the pelvic floor. The shiny fascia over the pelvic floor becomes visible as the dissection approaches the muscle.

In female patients, the surgeon suspends the uterus using a Keith needle through the fundus and employs a sponge stick in the vagina for retraction, aiding in the identification of the rectovaginal septum. Before stapling, the surgeon performs a digital check to confirm the dissection reaches the top of the anal canal and ensures the staple line does not include the back wall of the vagina.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to divide the mesentery, performing the division at a convenient location without concern for cancerous tissue.

The surgeon makes a cruciate incision in the anterior and posterior fascia at the right lower quadrant site, separating the muscle fibers. The entire specimen is extracted through this site, keeping the ileocolic pedicle intact.
The surgeon proceeds to transect the bowel and create a pouch. A drain is left in the pelvis, and one of the five millimeter port sites is repurposed as a drain site.
The patient has a ten millimeter incision just above the umbilicus, with two additional small incisions nearby to accommodate her navel ring.
So can we make it even less invasive? And I'm just going to show a little bit of a clip here because I don't want to steal from Dan Geisler's talk in a few moments.

So how about we just do the whole thing through the ileostomy site? There's a learning curve to this. We have to unlearn some of the lessons that we had to learn for laparoscopy. It's still possible to triangulate. It just is a little harder.

Use of articulated instruments makes things a little more difficult. We found a long laparoscope is helpful. It gets the scope out of the way and to either have a tall assistant to hold it or have them on a step also keeps them out of the way. Small movements are necessary because big movements move the camera. It's set up in the OR. It really requires cooperation of the whole team.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the colon through a single incision, bringing the mesentery out through the ileostomy site. The ileocolic artery is preserved while transecting the terminal ileum. A standard fifteen-centimeter J pouch is created and repositioned into the abdomen, followed by the formation of a circular stapled anastomosis. The dissection is performed deep in the pelvis.

The surgeon ensures the mesentery lays straight before firing the stapler, with the camera positioned over the duodenum and retroperitoneum on the right side. The anastomosis is created, and an ileostomy is brought up.

We can stop the video and go back to the PowerPoint, please. So that was the specimen extracted through the ileostomy site and the final incision. So I'd like to thank you very much for your attention and allowing me to speak this afternoon. Thank you.
